content: Tickets for Lighting the Way are live. October 17 at the Grace Community Church hall, $50 a seat, with a goal of raising $15,000 for the year ahead. Bring people who like a good cause and a decent dinner.
excerpt: Tickets for the Lighting the Way gala are now open.
title: Gala tickets are open

content: A short, plain guide to becoming a volunteer with HFF. We cover the application, the background check, and what the first month actually looks like. No fluff, just the steps.
excerpt: Everything you need to start volunteering with HFF.
title: How to start volunteering

content: Three things went right at the winter coat drive and one thing we will fix next time. Sharing both, because the good and the awkward are usually worth the same amount.
excerpt: What worked, and what we will fix, at the winter coat drive.
title: Notes from the winter coat drive

content: Where Hartford Foster Futures is headed in 2027: more mentorship matches, a second resource fair, and steady work on the bills we have been tracking. Modest goals, done well.
excerpt: A look at our priorities for the year ahead.
title: Our 2027 priorities

content: Field notes from testimony day at the State Capitol. We spoke on the sibling placement bill, sat through a long afternoon, and came away cautiously hopeful. Here is what happened.
excerpt: A recap from a day of testimony at the Capitol.
title: Field notes from testimony day

content: A draft guide for foster families on the first week with a new placement. Still in progress, but the bones are here: the bedroom, the school records, and the slow part where you just let a kid settle in.
excerpt: A draft guide to the first week with a new placement.
title: The first week with a new placement

content: Every child who arrives at a new home deserves something that is theirs on day one. Here is what we pack into an HFF welcome kit: pajamas in their size, a soft blanket, a toothbrush, a small notebook, and a stuffed animal that nobody else has touched.
excerpt: What goes into an HFF welcome kit, and why each item matters.
title: Packing a welcome kit that feels like home

content: People picture mentoring as big outings, but the first month is quieter than that. It is a standing coffee, a text on a hard day, and showing up when you said you would. We walk through what a new match can actually expect.
excerpt: The first month of a mentor match is quieter than you think.
title: What mentors actually do in the first month

content: The sibling placement bill in front of the legislature would require agencies to keep brothers and sisters together whenever it is safe to do so, and to document the reasons when they cannot. Here is a plain-language breakdown of what it would change.
excerpt: A plain-language look at the sibling placement bill.
title: How the sibling placement bill would work

content: Over eleven years, the Alvarez house has been home to nine teenagers, some for a weekend and some for years. In this piece I share what we learned about patience, boundaries, and letting a kid keep their own story.
excerpt: One Hartford family on eleven years of fostering teens.
title: The family who fostered nine teenagers

content: At the aging-out resource fair, young people leaving care met with housing counselors, a credit union, and a few employers who actually hire at eighteen. I spent the morning at the sign-in table and left with a notebook full of ideas for next year.
excerpt: Scenes and lessons from the aging-out resource fair.
title: A morning at the aging-out resource fair

content: When a foster sibling joins the family, your own kids have questions and feelings too. We share the simple, honest scripts that helped our household make room without pretending everything was easy.
excerpt: Honest ways to prepare your children for a foster sibling.
title: Talking to your kids about a new foster sibling

content: Kelly and I started Hartford Foster Futures in 2024 because we kept seeing the same gaps: kids showing up with trash bags, families with no one to call at 9pm. This is the story of the first year and why we keep going.
excerpt: The story behind why we founded HFF in 2024.
title: Why we started Hartford Foster Futures

content: No, foster parents are not in it for the money. No, you do not need to own a house or be married. We take the five things people ask us most often and answer them straight, no jargon.
excerpt: The five questions we hear most, answered plainly.
title: Five myths about foster care, answered

content: The winter coat drive looks smooth on the day, but the two weeks before it are all spreadsheets and folding tables. Here is what actually goes into collecting, sorting, and handing out four hundred coats.
excerpt: The unglamorous work behind a smooth coat drive.
title: Behind the scenes of the winter coat drive

content: A gala table seats eight and covers a season of welcome kits. We break down the three sponsor levels, what each one includes, and how the money maps to actual programs for Hartford youth.
excerpt: What a gala table sponsorship covers, level by level.
title: Sponsoring a gala table, explained

content: People imagine courtrooms from television. A real permanency hearing is mostly waiting, careful paperwork, and a judge asking whether a child has what they need. I write about a day on the job as a DCF social worker.
excerpt: A caseworker's honest account of a day in family court.
title: What a courtroom day looks like for a caseworker

content: Respite care is a planned, short break where another trained family takes a child for a weekend so the primary home can rest. It is not failure; it is how foster families keep going. Here is how respite works and how to sign up.
excerpt: How respite care keeps foster families going.
title: Respite care: the break every foster family needs

content: A workplace supply drive is one of the easiest ways to help, and it barely touches your day job. We share the one-page plan we hand out: pick a week, name a champion, post the list, and let us handle pickup.
excerpt: A simple one-page plan for a workplace supply drive.
title: How to run a supply drive at your workplace

content: Every Saturday morning a handful of volunteers set up tables at the West End library and tutor kids in care through math and reading. We spotlight the crew that has kept this going for a year and a half.
excerpt: Meet the volunteers behind our Saturday tutoring table.
title: Volunteer spotlight: the Saturday tutoring crew
